phases.pali: sattānaṁ visuddhiyā,

tips: √satt: from √as 'to be'; satta = a being, sentient creature
Related to sattva — what is real or existing
The -ānaṁ ending marks genitive plural: 'of the beings'
Pāli uses this suffix to show belonging — English uses 'of the'
Here it intensifies the root, meaning 'utterly pure'
The -yā ending is dative singular: 'for the purpose of'
Also used as instrumental: 'by means of' — context determines which

phases.pali: dukkhadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamāya,

tips: Pain — the first arrow; physical suffering
From duḥ (bad) + kha (space/axle): a wheel with a poorly fitting axle
Mental distress — the second arrow; psychological stress
From duḥ (bad) + manas (mind): a mind in a bad state
The -āna ending means 'of the [plural]' — Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself, where English would say 'of the'.
The -ṁ is part of the genitive plural ending -ānaṁ — it closes the vowel, like a period on the case.
Atthaṁ is a prefix meaning 'gone home, set (of sun)' — here: 'going to an end'
√gam: To go — the root behind 'come', 'go', 'path' in many Indo-European languages
The -ā before -ya lengthens the stem vowel — it's a sandhi bridge, not a separate morpheme
The -ya ending makes the noun dative — 'for the purpose of', 'in order to'
Pāli marks 'for' on the noun itself, where English uses a preposition

phases.pali: nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya,

tips: ni√vā → nibbāna: 'blowing out' of a flame
The -ssa ending means 'of the [singular]' — Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself, where English would say 'of the'.
[Stem] sacchi: before the eyes, in person
Common in compound verbs: 'to make visible'
Turns the verb into 'the act of...'
senses: for the sake of personally experiencing

phases.pali: yadidaṁ cattāro satipaṭṭhānā.

tips: yad + idaṁ = "namely, that is to say"
Sati: mindfulness, presence — the core practice faculty
The -i ending is part of the stem, not a suffix
paṭṭhāna: foundation, setting up — from pa (prefix) + √sthā (to stand)
[Nominative plural] The -ā ending marks masculine nominative plural — 'the [four] foundations'

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: vi-: Apart, away — marks removal or separation
Indicates an action completed before the main verb
loka: World, realm — the sphere of experience
Often means 'the world of beings' or 'society'
Pāli marks the domain directly on the noun
These two stand for ALL FIVE Hindrances:
abhi-: Toward, intensively — marks directed or excessive desire
√dhyai: To think, to covet — with jhā as the Pali development
Accusative singular neuter ending: 'regarding covetousness and displeasure'
Marks what is removed by the gerund vineyya
Idha: Here — in this teaching or context
Marks the start of the instruction proper
Root underlying 'mendicant': one who shares in alms
The -e ending calls out to a group: 'O monks!'
Root √bhikkh: the mendicant — one who lives on alms
Now nominative singular, the subject of the sentence
The -e ending is locative singular: 'in the body'
kāya: body — same word as in p4, now in compound
The long ā is from sandhi fusion with the next part
√paś: To see — with nū- prefix meaning 'along with, closely'
vi-: Apart, intensively — marks a separated or intensified dwelling
√har: To carry, to take — originally 'to carry oneself along'
In Buddhist Pāli: 'to dwell, to abide'
√tap: To burn, to heat — ascetic 'heat' of effort
Buddhist: burning away defilements through practice
sam-: Together, completely — intensifies the knowing
√jñā: To know — from the same root as 'knowledge' in English
Not just memory but present-moment awareness

phases.pali: vedanāsu vedanānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: √tap: To burn, to heat — from Vedic tapas (ascetic heat)
Buddhist sense: burning of defilements through effort
The ī ending is a possessive suffix — 'one who has ardor'
Same pattern as satimā, vedanānupassī: stem + ī = 'possessing the quality of the stem'
Prefix sam-: together, completely — intensifies the root that follows
Prefix pa-: forth, forward — directional, like 'bringing knowledge forth'
√jñā: To know — the root of 'jānāti' (to know)
With sam+pa it forms sampajāna — 'complete and active knowing', often translated 'clearly aware'
The -o ending marks nominative singular masculine — 'the one who is...'
Agrees with the implied subject (bhikkhu) in gender and number
√smṛ: To remember — the root of sati (mindfulness, presence)
Sati in Buddhism means 'presence of mind', not just memory
The -mā suffix is a contracted form of -mant — 'possessing'
Same pattern as guṇavant (possessing virtues) but with -mā: satimā = 'one who possesses mindfulness'
The stem vedanā — felt experience, feeling tone
Behind the surface 'vedanāsu' is the feminine noun vedanā (feeling), which appears here in the locative plural
The stem vedanā — same word as in the previous phrase, now forming a compound
√paś: To see, to behold — the root of 'passati' (to see)
With the prefix 'anupass' it means 'contemplating' or 'observing closely'
Prefix vi-: apart, intensively — here it gives a sense of 'passing time' or 'continuing'
Same prefix as in vikkhambhetvā (dispersed) and vibhajati (analyzes)
√hṛ: To carry, to bear — the root of 'harati' (to carry)
Prefix vi-: apart, away — here it means 'removing' or 'setting aside'
√nī: To lead — the root of 'neti' (to lead)
With vi- it forms vineyya — to lead away, to remove (from the mind)
√lok: The world — from √luk (to see) via 'that which is seen'
In Buddhism, loka can mean the world of beings, the cosmos, or a plane of existence
The -e ending marks locative singular — 'in the world'
The stem abhijjhā — covetousness, greed, longing
Literally 'desire (jhāna) turned toward (abhi-)' — the mind's craving
The stem domanassa — displeasure, aversion, mental pain

phases.pali: citte cittānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: Not thoughts — the STATE of consciousness
The stem of 'citta' — mind, heart, thought. Here it's in the locative case.
The -e ending means 'in/on/at' — Pāli marks location on the noun itself, where English uses prepositions.
The stem 'citta' (mind) with a lengthened vowel due to sandhi before the following 'anupassī'.
Part of the prefix 'anu' (along, according to) — shortened after a vowel. Together with 'passī' it means 'observing'.
From √pass (to see) + suffix -ī (one who has). 'passī' means 'seeing' or 'observing'. So 'cittānupassī' = 'one who observes the mind'.
Prefix vi- means 'apart, separate' — here it gives the sense of 'dwelling in a state of separation' (from defilements).
√har: to carry, to take. With vi- it means 'to dwell' (lit. to carry oneself apart).
Thematic vowel a — connects the root to the personal ending.
The -ti ending marks third person singular present tense: 'he/she dwells'.
√tap: to burn, to heat. In Buddhist context, 'ardor' or 'ascetic effort' — burning away defilements.
The -ī ending is a possessive suffix: 'one who has...'. So 'ātāpī' = 'one who has ardor'.
√jñā: to know. The form 'jān' is the present stem. Together with prefixes, it means 'knowing thoroughly' or 'clearly aware'.
Sati: mindfulness, memory, presence of mind. From √smṛ (to remember).
The -mā ending is from the possessive suffix -mant (contracted): 'one who possesses...'. So 'satimā' = 'one who possesses mindfulness'.
Prefix vi-: apart, away. Here it gives the sense of 'removing' or 'getting rid of'.
From √nī (to lead) + gerund suffix -ya. 'neyya' is the gerund form meaning 'having led' or 'having removed'. Together: 'vineyya' = 'having removed'.
Stem of 'loka' — world, realm, the world of experience.
The -e ending is locative singular: 'in the world'. Pāli marks location on the noun.
Abhijjhā: covetousness, longing. From abhi (over) + √jhā (to desire) + ā.
Domanassa: displeasure, aversion. From du (bad) + manas (mind) + a (suffix). Literally 'bad-mindedness'.
The -ṁ ending is accusative singular neuter: marks the object of the action. 'abhijjhādomanassaṁ' = 'covetousness and displeasure' (as object).

phases.pali: citte cittānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: √tap: To burn, heat. In Buddhist context, the ascetic 'heat' that burns defilements — as in tapas (austerity).
The -ī suffix is a possessive ending — 'one who possesses ardor'. Nominative singular masculine.
Prefix sam-: together, completely — it intensifies the root that follows.
Prefix pa- (from pra): forward, forth — adds a directional sense: 'knowing thoroughly'.
√jñā: To know — the Pāli verb 'jānāti' (to know). This cluster (sam+pa+jān) means to know with full clarity and comprehension.
The -o ending is nominative singular masculine for thematic stems — agreeing with the subject bhikkhu.
√smṛ: To remember. Sati means mindfulness — a capacity for presence and non-forgetting, not just memory.
The suffix -mā (from -mant, contracted) is possessive — 'possessing mindfulness'. Nominative singular masculine.
The stem 'citta' means mind, heart, or thought — the inner experiencing faculty, not the brain.
The -e ending is locative singular — 'in/on/with reference to'. Pāli marks location on the noun itself, where English would say 'in the mind'.
The stem 'citta' — mind — with its final vowel lengthened to ā before the compound second member.
Contracted form of anupassī: 'one who observes closely'. Comes from 'anu' (following) + 'passati' (see).
The -ī ending is a possessive suffix in nominative singular — 'one who has/does this'. Masculine, agreeing with bhikkhu.
Prefix vi- often means 'apart, intensive, special'. Here it adds a sense of continued, settled dwelling (not just staying).
√har: To carry, take, or, when combined with vi-, to abide/dwell — the verb of settled presence in a place or state.
The thematic vowel -a- is a stem-building element, marking this as a present-tense verb form.
Prefix vi-: apart, separation. Here combined with the root √nī, it means to remove or put aside.
√nī: To lead. With vi- prefix, 'to lead away' = to remove. The -ey- indicates an absolutive (gerund) form — 'having removed'.
The -yya suffix marks the absolutive (gerund): a completed action that precedes the main verb — 'having removed'.
Stem 'loka' means world, realm of existence, or the beings in it.
The -e ending is locative singular — 'in the world'. Pāli marks location on the noun itself.
Prefix abhi-: toward, over, intensifying. It points outward (toward objects) in 'abhijjhā' (covetousness).
√jhā (or √dhyai): To think, desire. 'Abhijjhā' means covetousness — desire reaching out toward things.
The stem 'domanassa' means displeasure, mental pain — from 'du' (bad) + 'manas' (mind). The compound functions as a dvandva: 'covetousness and displeasure'.

phases.pali: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.

tips: dhamma: teaching, principles, phenomena. The -e ending makes it 'in/among the dhammas' — Pāli marks location on the noun, where English uses a preposition.
The -su ending means 'in/among [plural]' — locative plural. Marks the context or domain where something happens.
dhamma: teaching, principles, phenomena. Here the stem is lengthened (dhammā) before the compound second member.
nupassī: 'one who observes' — from anu (following) + √paś (to see). The compound means 'observing dhammas'.
anu-: following, along with. Changes to nupa- before the root.
√paś: To see. Related to English 'scope' and 'spectacle'. Anupassī = one who sees closely, observes.
vi-: intensive prefix, can mean 'apart' or 'in various ways'
√hṛ: To carry, to bring. Extended sense: 'to remain, to dwell'.
-ati ending: third person singular present tense — 'he/she/it dwells'. Pāli uses the same form for ongoing actions.
√tap: To burn, to heat. Vedic tapas = ascetic heat. Buddhist: burning of defilements.
The -ī ending makes a possessive adjective: 'one who has ardor'. Nominative singular.
sam-: together, completely. Intensifies the action.
√jñā: To know. Cognate with English 'know' and Greek 'gnosis'. Sampajañña = clear comprehension.
√smṛ: To remember. Sati = mindfulness, presence. Cognate with 'smriti' (Hindu tradition).
The -mā ending (from -mant) makes a possessive adjective: 'one who possesses mindfulness'. Nominative singular.
vi-: apart, intensive. Combined with √nī (to lead) to mean 'remove, get rid of'.
vi-: intensive prefix, here meaning 'apart, away'.
√nī: To lead. The -eyya ending is a gerund (absolutive): 'having led away' → 'having removed'. Pāli uses gerunds for completed actions that precede the main verb.
loka: world, people. The -e ending means 'in/for the world' — locative singular. Same stem as in 'loka' (world).
The -e ending is locative singular: 'in the world'. Pāli marks location on the noun, not with a separate preposition.
abhijjhā: covetousness, greed. From abhi (over) + √jhā (to think) — 'thinking excessively about'.
abhi-: over, toward. Intensifies the root idea.
√jhā: To think, to meditate. Cognate with 'dhyāna' (absorption meditation).

phases.pali: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.

tips: √tap: To burn, heat. ātāpī means 'ardent' — the 'burning' of defilements through effort. Related to Vedic tapas (ascetic heat).
The -ī suffix marks a possessive adjective: 'one who has ardor'. Nominative singular masculine.
sam-: together, completely. Combined with pa- and √jñā, it intensifies 'knowing' to mean 'fully comprehending'.
pa-: forth, forward (from pra-). With √jñā 'to know', it becomes 'clearly knowing'.
√jñā: To know. The stem jāna is the present stem; -o is the nominative singular ending. Related to English 'know' (cognate through Indo-European).
Dhamma: principles, phenomena, teachings — the root √dhṛ means 'to uphold'. Here in the locative case: 'among/in relation to principles'.
The -su ending is locative plural: 'among/within'. Pāli marks the domain of observation on the noun itself, where English says 'an aspect of' or 'among'.
Dhamma again: the same root √dhṛ, now in compound form. The long ā shows it is in the stem form used before -anupassī.
Anupassī comes from anu (following/along) + √paś (to see). 'Observing along/tracking' — continuous close attention.
The -ī suffix marks a possessive adjective: 'one who observes'. Nominative singular masculine.
vi-: a prefix meaning 'apart, separated, intensive'. Here it adds nuance to √hṛ, often indicating a state of being that is extended or clearly distinguished.
√hṛ: To take, carry, bear — but in viharati it means 'to dwell' or 'abide', like 'bearing oneself'.
The thematic vowel -a- is the glue between root and ending. It is required for the present tense system of this verb class.

phases.pali: Uddeso niṭṭhito.

tips: The brief statement before detailed explanation
uddesa: 'summary' or 'synopsis' — literally 'pointing up', from ud-√dis (to point out, indicate). In this text, the 'outline' section that has just been recited.
[Nominative singular] The -o ending makes uddesa the subject of the clause — 'the summary [is]...'. Pāli marks the subject with -o for masculine nouns, where English just uses word order.
niṭṭhā: 'finished, complete' — the root is ni√sthā (to stand still, be settled). The passive sense 'was finished' or 'has been concluded' comes from the suffix.

[Past participle] The -ta ending turns the verb into an adjective describing a completed state — 'finished'. Pāli uses this same form for both the act (has been finished) and the resulting state (is finished).
[Nominative singular masculine] The -o ending agrees with uddeso — 'the outline is finished'. In Pāli, the past participle agrees in gender, number, and case with the subject.

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu araññagato vā rukkhamūlagato vā suññāgāragato vā nisīdati, pallaṅkaṁ ābhujitvā, ujuṁ kāyaṁ paṇidhāya, parimukhaṁ satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā.

tips: Here — in this teaching, in this context
The same stem as bhikkhu below, now in vocative
[Vocative plural] 'Hey you all!' — calling out to an assembled group
A bhikkhu is one who lives on alms — a mendicant
A forested area away from villages
This is the past participle: 'has gone to'
[Nominative singular masculine] Agreeing with bhikkhu — 'the monk gone to the forest'
The shaded spot at the base of a tree
senses: at the foot of a tree
to the base of a tree
gone to the root of a tree

tips: Full stem: suñña (final vowel elided in sandhi before āgāra)
Related to Latin sedeo — to sit
[Adverbial] Describes how the body is placed
From √kāś: To shine, appear — the body as 'appearance'
[Prefix] ni-: Down, into (with nasalization due to sandhi)
√dhā: To put, to place, to set
parimukhaṁ: In front, as first priority — lit. 'around the front'
[Indeclinable] Often in meditation instruction: 'to the fore'
sati: Mindfulness — the faculty of present awareness
Root often seen in 'healthy' (cf. English thermostat, stable)

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu araññagato vā rukkhamūlagato vā suññāgāragato vā nisīdati, pallaṅkaṁ ābhujitvā, ujuṁ kāyaṁ paṇidhāya, parimukhaṁ satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā.

tips: The -ati ending is 3rd person singular present tense — 'he sits down'
Can mean full lotus or seated posture
pallaṅka: 'cross-legged position' — the meditation posture, also means 'couch'
Accusative singular — 'having bent the cross-legged position' (i.e., 'having sat cross-legged')
Absolutive suffix — 'having bent' (an action completed before the next)
ujuṁ: 'straight', 'erect' — adverbial form modifying how the body is placed
kāya: 'body' — physical body, from √kṛ: to do/make
Accusative singular — 'the body' (direct object of paṇidhāya)
Paṇidhāya = having placed forward, having directed
√dhā: To place, to put. With prefix: 'to position'
Idha: 'Here' — sets the scene (in this teaching, in this context)
The -e ending is vocative plural — calling out to the group: 'O mendicants!'
From √bhikkh: to beg/share. The root is related to religious mendicancy, not poverty.
Nominative singular: 'a mendicant' (subject of the sentence)
arañña: 'forest' or 'wilderness' — a remote place, away from villages
√gam: To go (here: gata = 'gone', past participle). 'Gone to the forest'
Nominative singular masculine — describes the bhikkhu
parimukhaṁ: 'in front', 'to the fore' — often technical in meditation instructions: 'in the area around the mouth' or 'as first priority'
√smṛ: To remember. sati = mindfulness, presence of mind
Accusative singular feminine — 'mindfulness' (direct object of upaṭṭhapetvā)
√sthā: To stand — with upa-: 'to stand near, to establish'
Causative marker — 'to cause to stand' = 'to establish'

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu araññagato vā rukkhamūlagato vā suññāgāragato vā nisīdati, pallaṅkaṁ ābhujitvā, ujuṁ kāyaṁ paṇidhāya, parimukhaṁ satiṁ upaṭṭhapetvā.

tips: Parimukha: 'around the front, as first priority' — from pari (around) + mukha (mouth/face). Often means 'directed toward the meditation object'.
The -ṁ ending is an adverbial suffix — 'as first priority, to the fore'.
Sati: 'mindfulness, presence' — from √smṛ (to remember). Related to English 'mind', 'memory'.
The -ṁ ending is accusative singular — the direct object: 'mindfulness' (what is established).
Upaṭṭhapeti = to establish, set up, make present
The prefix 'upa-' means 'toward, near, onto' — directional.
√sthā: 'to stand, establish' — related to English 'stand', Latin 'stare'. Causative stem here: 'to cause to stand, set up'.
The -etvā suffix is the causative absolutive — 'having caused to stand, having established'. This is the final preparatory action before the main verb (sitting).
Idha: 'Here' — referring to this teaching or context.
The root 'bhikkh' means to share or beg — a bhikkhu is one who lives on alms, a mendicant.
The -av- suffix is part of the stem for the vocative plural form of 'bhikkhu'.
The -e ending is vocative plural — calling out to a group: 'O mendicants!' Pāli marks the addressee on the noun itself.
Bhikkhu: 'mendicant, monk' — one who lives on alms. Same root as bhikkhave above, now in nominative singular.
√gam: 'to go' — the root appears here as 'ga' (weakened form in compounds).
The -t suffix makes the verb past participle: 'gone'. Araññagato = 'gone to the forest'.
Vā: 'or' — a disjunctive particle linking alternatives.
Mūla: 'root, foot' — the base of a tree.
√gam: 'to go' — same root as in araññagato.
Vā: 'or' — completing the series of alternatives.
The prefix 'ni-' means 'down' — adds a sense of 'downward' or 'settling'.
√sad: 'to sit, settle' — related to Latin 'sedēre' (to sit).
The -ati ending is third-person singular present tense — 'he sits down'. This is the main verb of this phase.
Pallaṅka: 'cross-legged sitting posture' — the meditation seat.
The -ṁ ending is accusative singular — the direct object: 'cross-legged posture' (what is bent/folded).
The prefix 'ā-' means 'toward, back' — directional intensifier.
√bhuj: 'to bend, fold' — related to 'bend', 'bow' in English. Here used for crossing the legs.
The -itvā suffix is the absolutive — like '-ing' in English, but it marks a completed action before the main verb: 'having bent/folded' (the legs, then sitting).
The -ṁ ending is an adverbial suffix — 'straightly, erectly'. Makes the adjective into an adverb modifying the posture.
The -ṁ ending is accusative singular — the direct object: 'body' (what is set straight).
√dhā: 'to place, set' — 'paṇidhā' = 'to place forth, direct'. The nasal 'ṇ' is a sandhi effect from pa+dhā.
The -ya ending is the absolutive — 'having placed/set'. Another completed action before the main verb: 'having set the body straight'.

phases.pali: So satova assasati, satova passasati.

tips: Pronoun 'he' (nominative singular) — refers to the meditator; often left untranslated in English
Prefix from ā: 'toward, back'. In composition with 'assasati', it means 'in' (breathing in)
√sās: 'to breathe' — the root underlying 'breath' words (assāsa, passāsa). The double 'ss' comes from sandhi
Thematic vowel -a- + present stem marker -sa-: builds the present stem for this verb class
3rd person singular present ending: 'he/she/it does'. So assasati = 'he breathes in'
Prefix pa: 'forth, out'. Contrasts with ā- in assasati: pa- means 'out' (breathing out), ā- means 'in'
√sās: 'to breathe' — same root as in assasati. The pair assasati/passasati forms the standard Pali compound for 'breathing in and out'
Present stem suffix -sa- for this verb class, same as in assasati
3rd person singular present ending: 'he breathes out'. Parallel structure to assasati
Stem of satimant: 'mindful' (nominative singular masculine). The -mant possessive suffix indicates 'one who possesses mindfulness'
Sandhi form of eva — emphatic particle: 'just', 'only', 'simply'. Together with sato it means 'just mindful'
Stem of satimant: 'mindful' (nominative singular masculine) — same form as in p2, repeated for rhythm
Emphatic particle eva in sandhi: 'just', 'only'. The repeated pattern 'satova ... satova' mirrors the Pali refrain structure

phases.pali: rassaṁ vā assasanto ‘rassaṁ assasāmī’ti pajānāti, rassaṁ vā passasanto ‘rassaṁ passasāmī’ti pajānāti.

tips: [Adverb] Lightly, for a short time — modifies the breathing
[Adverbial neuter ending] Makes the adjective into an adverb: 'lightly'
[Particle] Or — used to introduce an alternative case
[Root] √śvas: To breathe — the underlying action for 'inhaling'
[Thematic vowel] Connects the root to the participle ending
[Present participle, masculine nominative singular] 'One who is ...-ing' — describes the subject doing the action
[Adverb] Lightly — same as p1, repeated in the second clause
[Adverbial neuter ending] Same function as p1s2
[Prefix] Forth, forward — directional prefix for exhalation
[Root] √śvas: To breathe — same root as p3, but with 'pa-' prefix
[Present participle, masculine nominative singular] Same form as p3s4 — 'one who is exhaling'
[Punctuation] Opening quotation mark — marks the start of direct speech
[Adverb] Lightly — same form as p1, repeated in the quotation
[Verb stem] Breathe in — from 'a-śvas' with long vowel for 1st person singular
[1st person singular ending] 'I' — marks the speaker as Ānanda or the monk
[Punctuation] Closing quote mark (elision of 'iti') — ends the direct speech
[Prefix] Pa-: Forth, forward — intensifies the verb
[Root] √jñā: To know — the core cognitive verb
[Thematic vowel] Long ā before the personal ending in present tense
[Verb stem] Breathe out — from 'pa-śvas' with long vowel for 1st person singular
[1st person singular ending] 'I' — same as p5s2
[Punctuation] Closing quote mark — same as p5s3
[Prefix] Pa-: Forth, forward — same as p6s1

phases.pali: ‘Sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī assasissāmī’ti sikkhati, ‘sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī passasissāmī’ti sikkhati.

tips: √sikkh: To learn, to practice — related to 'sikkhā' (training)
Thematic vowel a — connects root to personal ending
The -ti ending is 3rd person singular — 'he/she practices'
√sikkh: To learn, to practice — related to 'sikkhā' (training)
Thematic vowel a — connects root to personal ending
The -ti ending is 3rd person singular — 'he/she practices'
Paṭi-: Prefix meaning 'back, against, each' — often indicates reciprocal or intensive action
Assas: 'Breathe in' — from ā + √śvas. The act of inhalation.
The -iss- suffix marks the future tense — 'will [do]'
The -āmī ending is 1st person singular — 'I will' — combined with 'ti' enclitic
Paṭi-: Prefix meaning 'back, against, each' — often indicates reciprocal or intensive action
Passas: 'Breathe out' — from pa + √śvas. The act of exhalation.
The -iss- suffix marks the future tense — 'will [do]'
The -āmī ending is 1st person singular — 'I will' — combined with 'ti' enclitic

phases.pali: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, dakkho bhamakāro vā bhamakārantevāsī vā dīghaṁ vā añchanto ‘dīghaṁ añchāmī’ti pajānāti, rassaṁ vā añchanto ‘rassaṁ añchāmī’ti pajānāti;

tips: Vedic dakṣa, Greek δεξιός, Latin decet
📝 The turner is not a passive
bhamakāra: turner, lathe operator — a carpenter who works a lathe
bhamakārantevāsī: turner's assistant, apprentice lathe operator
A long, slow pull on the lathe
añchanta: present participle — 'turning, cutting (on a lathe)'
Seyyathā: 'It is just as if' — phrase introducing a simile
pi: Emphatic particle — 'even, just' — reinforces the comparison
📢 (Vocative plural) 'O monks!' — the Buddha addressing the assembly
añchāmi: first-person singular — 'I am turning / cutting'
pajānāti: he knows clearly — parallel to the earlier verb

phases.pali: ‘sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī assasissāmī’ti sikkhati, ‘sabbakāyapaṭisaṁvedī passasissāmī’ti sikkhati;

tips: √śikṣ: To learn, to train — the root of 'sikkhā' (training) and 'sekha' (trainee).
The -a-: Thematic vowel used to form present tense stems.
The -ti: Third person singular present tense ending — 'he/she/it trains'.
Punctuation: semi-colon separating two parallel clauses.
√śikṣ: To learn/train — same root as p3s1; the repetition underscores 'training' as the core of this practice.
Same third person singular present as p3s3; the entire clause repeats with the out-breath.
Punctuation: semi-colon ends the paired clause, concluding the whole teaching segment.
Sabba: All, every, whole — shows the scope of awareness is comprehensive, not partial.
Kāya: Body — the physical collection of parts, the whole body in Pāli thought.
Paṭi-: Prefix meaning 'back, against, in return, toward' — here it directs attention toward the body.
A-: Prefix meaning 'toward' — here it indicates the direction of the breath, inward.
√śvas: To breathe — the root common to 'assāsa' (in-breath) and 'passāsa' (out-breath).
The -i-: Thematic vowel that connects the root to the future ending.
Sabba: All, every, whole — repeated term showing structured, balanced phrasing.
Kāya: Body — same as p1s2; identical compound structure in the second clause.
Paṭi-: Same prefix as p1s3; the symmetry emphasizes the paired in/out breath practice.
Pa-: Prefix meaning 'forth, away' — distinguishes the out-breath from the in-breath (a-).
√śvas: To breathe — same root as p2s2; the symmetry of in and out breathing is a core pattern.
The -i-: Thematic vowel, same function as p2s3.

phases.pali: Iti ajjhattaṁ vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati, bahiddhā vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati, ajjhattabahiddhā vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati;

tips: Bahiddhā: An indeclinable meaning 'externally' or 'outside'—the opposite of ajjhattaṁ, referring to others' bodies.
Vā: 'or'—the third option in the series.
Kāyā: Sandhi stem for 'body' in compound.
√pass: To see closely—anu-prefix: 'following with the eye'.
Iti: An indeclinable particle meaning 'thus' or 'like this'—it introduces or summarizes a statement, like English quotation marks or 'and so'.
Ajjhattaṁ: An indeclinable meaning 'internally' or 'personally'—refers to one's own inner experience, opposed to 'externally'.
Vā: A particle meaning 'or'—it marks alternatives, here creating a progression: 'internally, or externally'.
Kāya: The stem 'kāya' means 'body'—the physical body or group. Here in the locative case, meaning 'in the body'.
The -e ending marks the locative case singular—'in/on/at' a location. Pāli marks location on the noun itself; English uses a preposition.
Kāyā: Modified stem of 'kāya' (body) before compounding—the long ā is a sandhi form, linking to 'anupassī'.
The root '√pass' means 'to see'—anu- adds the sense of 'following' or 'observing closely'. The whole compound means 'observing the body'.
The -ī suffix is a possessive ending: 'one who observes'. It makes the compound an adjective describing the meditator.
Vi-: A prefix meaning 'apart' or 'intensive'—here it adds emphasis to the root meaning 'to dwell'.
√har: To carry, to go—related to 'hari' (sun). With vi-, it means 'to carry on' or 'dwell' in a meditative sense.
The -a- is the thematic vowel, linking the root to the personal ending—a structural marker of present tense.
Vā: Again the particle 'or'—here pairing with the first vā to frame alternatives.
Kāya: Same stem as p4s1—'body', now again in the locative, indicating the perspective for observation.
The -e ending: locative singular—'in the body'.
Kāyā: Same sandhi stem as p5s1—'body' in the compound 'body-observation'.
√pass: To see—again with anu- prefix, meaning 'observing closely'.
Vi-: Intensive prefix, same as p6s1—emphasizing the dwelling.
√har: To dwell, carry on—same root as p6s2.
Ajjhattabahiddhā: A compound indeclinable meaning 'internally and externally'—combining both perspectives, the third alternative.

phases.pali: Iti ajjhattaṁ vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati, bahiddhā vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati, ajjhattabahiddhā vā kāye kāyānupassī viharati;

tips: Adverbial compound — 'internally and externally', 'both within and without'
Combines ajjhatta (internal) + bahiddhā (external) into one adverb
Indeclinable particle — 'or' — marks the third option in a series
'kāya' stem: body — the physical body, the object of observation
The -e locative singular ending: 'in the body', 'with respect to the body'
anu-√paś: to see, observe — 'looking along', 'watching continuously'
Possessive -ī suffix, nominative singular: 'one who observes'
√hṛ: to carry, to take — 'to move about', 'to dwell'
Thematic vowel a — marks present tense
3rd person singular present active ending: 'he/she/it dwells', 'meditates'
Indeclinable — a quotative particle: 'thus', 'this is', 'like this'
Often frames the description that follows: 'it is like this'
Here begins a series of options: internally OR externally OR both
'kāya' stem: body — the physical body, the object of observation
The -e locative singular ending: 'in the body', 'with respect to the body'
Pāli marks location on the noun itself where English needs 'in/at/on'
'kāya' stem with sandhi lengthening: 'body' — here the compound's first member
anu-√paś: to see, observe — 'looking along', 'watching continuously'
Possessive -ī suffix, nominative singular: 'one who observes', 'characterized by observing'
Prefix vi-: 'apart', 'through', 'out' — intensifies the root's meaning
√hṛ: to carry, to take — 'to move about', 'to dwell'
Thematic vowel a — bridges root to ending, marks present tense
Indeclinable particle — 'or' — marks the second option in a series
'kāya' stem: body — the physical body, the object of observation
The -e locative singular ending: 'in the body', 'with respect to the body'
anu-√paś: to see, observe — 'looking along', 'watching continuously'
Possessive -ī suffix, nominative singular: 'one who observes'
√hṛ: to carry, to take — 'to move about', 'to dwell'
Thematic vowel a — marks present tense

phases.pali: samudayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati, vayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati, samudayavayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati.

tips: Full stem: dhamma (final vowel elided in sandhi before ānu)
Vaya: from √vṛ or √gam meaning 'to go away, disappear' — vaya = 'going to ruin, passing away'
Dhammā: phenomena, things, or principles — here the nature of disappearance
The root √paś: to see — with prefix anu- meaning 'following'; anupassī = 'seeing in accordance with'
The -ī ending makes it a possessive adjective: 'one who sees...'
senses: viewing as subject to falling away

tips: Vā: 'or' — an indeclinable particle offering alternatives
Kāya: 'body' — the physical body or collection of parts
The -smiṁ ending marks the locative case: 'in', 'on', 'at'
Provides the location where the meditation occurs
[Prefix] Vi-: apart, intensive — often intensifies the root meaning
The root √har: to carry, to take — carries the sense of 'to abide, dwell'
The thematic vowel -a- that links the root to the personal ending, typical for present tense verbs
The -ti ending = 3rd person singular present tense: 'he/she abides'
Present tense — the action is happening now
[Prefix] Together, completely — often intensifies the root meaning
The root √ud-√i: to rise, come up — Udaya means 'arising' or 'coming into being'
Dhammā: phenomena, things, or principles — here in the sense of 'nature' or 'quality of'

Kāya: 'body' — the physical body or collection of parts
The -smiṁ ending marks the locative case: 'in', 'on', 'at'
Provides the location where the meditation occurs
[Prefix] Vi-: apart, intensive — often intensifies the root meaning
The root √har: to carry, to take — carries the sense of 'to abide, dwell'
The thematic vowel -a- that links the root to the personal ending, typical for present tense verbs
Vā: 'or' — an indeclinable particle offering alternatives
Kāya: 'body' — the physical body or collection of parts
The -smiṁ ending marks the locative case: 'in', 'on', 'at'
Provides the location where the meditation occurs
[Prefix] Vi-: apart, intensive — often intensifies the root meaning
The root √har: to carry, to take — carries the sense of 'to abide, dwell'

[Prefix] Together, completely — often intensifies the root meaning
The root √ud-√i: to rise, come up — Udaya means 'arising' or 'coming into being'
Vaya: from √vṛ or √gam meaning 'to go away, disappear' — vaya = 'going to ruin, passing away'

phases.pali: samudayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati, vayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati, samudayavayadhammānupassī vā kāyasmiṁ viharati.

tips: Full stem: dhamma (final vowel elided in sandhi before ānu)
dhamma: phenomena, nature, truth — here: 'things that are liable to'
anu-√paś: to see, contemplate — 'anupassī' means 'one who sees/observes'
Suffix -ī: possessive, 'one who has' — here: 'one who contemplates'
senses: contemplating both arising and disappearance in
seeing both origination and dissolution in
observing the nature of both arising and vanishing in

tips: Suffix -smiṁ: locative singular, 'in/on/at' — here: 'in the body'
Thematic vowel a — present tense stem
Suffix -ti: third person singular present, 'he/she/it does'
Prefix sam-: together, completely — here as part of 'samudaya': arising
√ud-√i: to rise up, come forth — 'udaya' means arising, origination
dhamma: phenomena, nature, truth — here: 'things that are liable to'
Suffix -smiṁ: locative singular, 'in/on/at' — here: 'in the body'
Prefix vi-: apart, intensive — here: intensifies 'to be'
√har (√hṛ): to carry, bear — 'viharati' means 'carries on, dwells, abides'
Thematic vowel a — carries the present tense stem
√vay (√vṛ): to disappear, fall away — 'vaya' means disappearance, decay
dhamma: phenomena, nature, truth — here: 'things that are liable to'
anu-√paś: to see, contemplate — 'anupassī' means 'one who sees/observes'
Suffix -smiṁ: locative singular, 'in/on/at' — here: 'in the body'
Thematic vowel a — present tense stem

phases.pali: ‘Atthi kāyo’ti vā panassa sati paccupaṭṭhitā hoti. Yāvadeva ñāṇamattāya paṭissatimattāya anissito ca viharati, na ca kiñci loke upādiyati.

tips: Yāva: As far as / To the extent
yāva: as far as; until. yāvadeva = 'just as far as'.
Not for ego, not for becoming
matta: measure; amount. 'ñāṇamattā' = 'a measure of knowledge'.
Dative singular — 'for the purpose of a measure of knowledge'.
matta: measure; amount. 'paṭissatimattā' = 'a measure of mindfulness'.
Dative singular — 'for the purpose of a measure of mindfulness'.
Dative singular (sandhi doubling). Same function as p10s3.
senses: for a measure of continuous mindfulness

tips: ‘Atthi — quoted speech marker, then 'there is' (verb). It introduces a direct thought.
kāya: body; physical process; group. Here in nominative, subject of 'atthi'.
Quotation marker — closes the thought; signals 'the idea that...'
Closing quote (doubled due to sandhi). Signals end of quoted thought.
vā: or. Marks alternative framing — 'or [the thought]...'
pana: and; moreover; on the other hand. Connecting particle.
assa: to/for him (gen/dat sg of 'ima'). 'panassa' = 'and/but for him'.
sati: mindfulness; presence. Nominative singular — the subject that 'paccupaṭṭhitā hoti'.
paccu- = pati- + ud- : prefix meaning 'back, again, towards'
Past participle suffix — marks completed state. 'has been established'.
√bhū: to be, become. Hoti = 'is' (3rd person singular present).
Past participle ending in nominative singular masculine — 'one who is detached'.
√vihṛ: to dwell, abide. vi- prefix + √hṛ = 'to move about' → 'to dwell'.
√vihṛ: to dwell, abide (same root as viharati). The form 'viharati' = 3rd person singular present.
ca: and. Connects the two clauses.
kiñci: anything (from kimañci). Indefinite pronoun, object of 'upādiyati'.
√lok: to perceive; loka = 'world'. Locative: 'in the world'.
upa- + ā-: upward or intensive; combined with √dā — 'to take up'.
Thematic vowel -a- + mediopassive marker -ya-: passive or reflexive — 'is clung to'.

phases.pali: ‘Atthi kāyo’ti vā panassa sati paccupaṭṭhitā hoti. Yāvadeva ñāṇamattāya paṭissatimattāya anissito ca viharati, na ca kiñci loke upādiyati.

tips: a-: Negative prefix — 'not'. Privation of the following: 'not dependent'.
√sri: To lean, depend — the root of 'relying on'. With ni- it means 'settled upon'.
Masculine nominative singular — 'he, independent'. The subject of viharati.
ca: And — coordinating conjunction. Links the two clauses: 'independent AND dwells'.
na: Not — the standard negation particle in Pāli.
ca: And — same conjunction as p12. Pairs with na to mean 'and not'.
kiñ (root kim): What? — interrogative pronoun. With ci it becomes indefinite: 'anything'.
-ci: Indefinite suffix — makes kim into 'something, anything'.
√lok: To see, perceive — the root of 'world'. A 'world' is a domain of seeing/perceiving.
Locative singular — 'in the world'. The -e ending marks location: where the grasping would occur.
Full stem: upa (final vowel elided in sandhi before ādi)
Opening quotation mark — marks the start of a direct thought/recollection phrase.
√as: To be, to exist — the verb of being. From this root come all 'is/are/exist' forms.
Third-person singular present active — 'there is'. The -i ending makes it present tense.
kāya: body — the physical body, accumulation of parts. The same word is used for 'group' or 'collection'.
Elision of 'iti' — a quotative marker meaning 'thus'. Here it closes the direct thought: 'The body exists, thus.'
Final -i of 'iti' — marks the end of the quotation.
vā: Or — an alternative particle. Here it introduces a new possibility: 'Or mindfulness is established...'
pana: But, moreover — a discourse particle shifting focus. It marks a transition in thought.
assa: For him — dative/genitive singular of the pronoun 'that one'. Indicates the meditator.
sati: mindfulness — from √smṛ 'to remember'. In Buddhism, it means presence of mind, awareness.
pati-: Toward, against — directional prefix. Here it suggests 'standing nearby' or 'present'.
upa-: Toward, near — intensifies the sense of closeness.
√sthā: To stand — the root of 'standing'. With prefixes, it means 'stood near' or 'attended'.
hoti: Is — third-person singular present of √bhū 'to be'. Used here as a copula.
yāva: As far as, until — a relative adverb of extent.
deva: indeed — emphatic particle. Together with yāva it means 'only as much as'.
euphonic -va: inserted for sandhi — no independent meaning.
ñāṇa: knowledge, gnosis — from √jñā 'to know'. Wisdom in a direct, experiential sense.
matta: measure, amount — from √mā 'to measure'. Suggests a limited, just-sufficient quantity.
Feminine dative singular — 'for a measure of knowledge'. The dative shows purpose.
pati-: Toward, back — directional prefix. Here it can mean 'continuous' or 'toward recollection'.
sati: mindfulness — the same stem as p5. Here with prefix paṭi-, it suggests 'recollected mindfulness'.
matta: measure — same stem as p9s2. 'Enough of mindfulness.'
vi-: Intensifier — 'apart, thoroughly'. Intensifies the root meaning 'to move about'.
√hṛ: To carry, take — the root of 'moving about'. With vi- it means 'to dwell, abide'.
Thematic vowel a — links the root to the personal ending.
√dā: To give, to take — the root of 'giving'. With ā- it takes the sense of 'taking up, grasping'.
ya: passive/reflexive suffix — makes the verb 'takes up for oneself / clings.'

phases.pali: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kurūsu viharati kammāsadhammaṁ nāma kurūnaṁ nigamo.

tips: Links proper name to common noun
The -naṁ ending means 'of the' — Pāli marks 'belonging to' on the noun itself
"A town" — what we are talking about
Literally 'a going down to' — a market town
The town is the subject of the clause
Name of a country and its inhabitants

phases.pali: “bhikkhavo”ti.

tips: ˈbhikkh-: from the root √bhikkh, 'to share' or 'to beg' — a religious mendicant, not a pauper; someone who lives on received alms as a spiritual practice.
The -vo ending is vocative plural — used when calling out to a group respectfully, like 'O monks!'

phases.pali: Bhagavā etadavoca:

tips: Fortune, good luck; from √bhaj: to share
The root here means 'one who has fortune'
The -vā ending is nominative singular of the possessive suffix -vant (like -mant)
One who possesses fortune — the Sublime One
Same stem as in phase-b and phase-e, now as the speaker/subject
Sandhi glide consonant linking eta- and avoca
Prefix a-: a past-tense augment (like English 'did speak')

phases.pali: Katame cattāro?

tips: katama asks for selection from a known set
The -e ending marks masculine plural — asking about a group
Nominative plural masculine form of catur (four)

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: "Body in body" = seeing body AS body
Kāya: body — literally 'an accumulation', the physical form.
The -e ending is locative singular — 'in the body' or 'in regard to the body'. English uses 'in/on' here; Pāli marks it on the noun.
Identity shift: you become an observer
Kāya: body — same stem as p4, now as the first member of a compound.
√paś: to see, to behold. With anu- prefix means 'observing after' or 'watching closely.'
The -ī ending marks a possessive adjective — 'one who observes the body'. Same pattern as ātāpī (p7).
vi-: A prefix meaning 'apart, separate, intensively.' It adds force or distinction to the root.
√har: to take, to carry, to dwell. In Pāli, 'viharati' means 'dwells, abides, stays' — carrying oneself along in a state.
The thematic vowel -a- connects the root to the personal ending.
The -ti ending is third-person singular present — 'he/she dwells'. Descriptive, not past or future.
Idha: Here, now — in this teaching, in this context.
The -e ending is vocative plural — calling out to a group of monks. Same stem as 'bhikkhu' (p3).
From √bhikkh: to share, to beg — a 'mendicant', one who lives on alms.
From √tap: to burn, to heat. In Buddhism, 'ardent' means with the burning energy of practice — the heat that consumes defilements.
The -ī ending marks a possessive adjective — 'one who has ardor'. Same suffix as in kāyānupassī (p5).
sam-: A prefix meaning 'together, completely.' Here it combines with pa- to intensify 'knowing'.
pa-: A prefix meaning 'forth, forward.' Together sam-pa- means 'fully, thoroughly'.
√jñā: to know. Sampajāna means 'clearly knowing' — active, continuous comprehension, not static knowledge.
From √smṛ: to remember. Sati is mindfulness — not just memory, but present-moment awareness.
The -mant suffix (contracted to -mā) means 'one who possesses' — 'one who has mindfulness'.
vi-: A prefix meaning 'apart, separate.' Here it suggests removal or setting aside.
√nī: to lead. With vi- prefix, 'vineyya' means 'having led away' — i.e., removed, set aside.
The -yya suffix marks an absolutive (gerund) — 'having removed'. It describes a completed action that sets the stage for what follows.
Loka: world, realm of beings — from √luc: to shine, to be bright; the visible world.
The -e ending is locative singular — 'in the world'. Same ending as kāye (p4).
abhijjhā: covetousness, greed — from abhi (toward) + √dhyai (to think). 'Thinking toward' = wanting.
domanassa: displeasure, aversion — from du (bad, difficult) + manas (mind). 'Bad-mindedness'.
The -ṁ ending is accusative singular neuter — the object of the removal: 'covetousness and displeasure'.
